Content: A tourniquet should be applied 2-3 inches above the wound on the limb, never over a joint. Tighten until bleeding stops completely. Write the time of application on the tourniquet or skin using a marker. Do not remove the tourniquet once applied in the field — this decision is made by a medical officer only. Common Indian Army tourniquet: CAT (Combat Application Tourniquet). If no tourniquet is available, improvise with a belt or cloth and a stick to twist and tighten (windlass method).
Keywords: tourniquet, bleeding, hemorrhage, limb, first aid, combat casualty care, trauma

Content: For deep wounds where a tourniquet cannot be applied (e.g., groin, armpit, neck), pack the wound tightly with gauze or a hemostatic dressing. Push gauze deep into the cavity and apply firm continuous pressure for at least 3-5 minutes. Do not remove packing in the field. Hemostatic agents like QuikClot or HemCon may be issued to soldiers. After packing, apply a pressure dressing on top to maintain pressure.
Keywords: wound packing, junctional wound, hemostatic, gauze, QuikClot, deep wound, groin, neck wound

Content: A tension pneumothorax occurs when air accumulates in the chest cavity, collapsing the lung. Signs include: difficulty breathing, absent breath sounds on one side, trachea shifted away from the affected side, dropping blood pressure, distended neck veins. Treatment in the field: needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line using a 14G needle. A chest seal (vented) should be applied to any open chest wound to prevent this condition.
Keywords: tension pneumothorax, chest wound, needle decompression, breathing difficulty, chest seal, sucking chest wound, lung collapse

Content: Soldiers are at risk of hypothermia in high-altitude postings like Siachen, Ladakh, or Kashmir. Symptoms progress: shivering (mild), confusion and loss of coordination (moderate), no shivering with altered consciousness (severe). Prevention: never sleep in wet clothes, use layered clothing systems, carry an emergency space blanket, eat high-calorie food regularly, stay hydrated. Treatment: remove wet clothing, insulate the casualty from the ground, apply warmth to the core (armpits, groin, neck), never rub the extremities.
Keywords: hypothermia, high altitude, Siachen, Ladakh, cold weather, shivering, temperature, mountain warfare

Content: For burns sustained from vehicle fires, IED blasts, or incendiary weapons: do not apply toothpaste, oil, or ice — these worsen injury. Cool the burn with clean cool (not cold) water for 20 minutes. Cover with a clean non-adhesive dressing. Elevate the affected area. Assess burn depth: superficial (redness), partial thickness (blisters), full thickness (white/charred, no pain). Burns covering more than 20% body surface area require immediate evacuation. Inhalation burns (singed nasal hair, hoarse voice) are life-threatening.
Keywords: burns, blast injury, IED, fire, inhalation injury, burn dressing, evacuation

Content: AMS occurs when ascending above 2,500m too quickly. Symptoms: headache, nausea, dizziness, fatigue, poor sleep. The most important rule is: never ascend with symptoms. Treatment: descend 300-500m, rest, hydration, Paracetamol or Ibuprofen for headache. Acetazolamide (Diamox) is used for prevention — 125-250mg twice daily starting 1-2 days before ascent. Indian Army postings in Ladakh, Kargil, and Arunachal Pradesh require mandatory acclimatization periods.
Keywords: AMS, altitude sickness, mountain sickness, acclimatization, Diamox, headache, Ladakh, Kargil, Siachen, high altitude

Content: HAPE is a life-threatening accumulation of fluid in the lungs at altitude. It is the leading cause of death from altitude illness. Symptoms: breathlessness at rest, dry cough progressing to pink frothy sputum, crackling sound in lungs, cyanosis (blue lips/fingertips). HAPE can develop with or without prior AMS. Immediate treatment: descend at least 1000m urgently, administer supplemental oxygen, Nifedipine 30mg extended release if available. Do not let the casualty exert themselves during descent.
Keywords: HAPE, altitude, pulmonary edema, breathlessness, oxygen, Nifedipine, high altitude, lung fluid, Siachen

Content: HACE is brain swelling due to altitude and is the most severe form of altitude illness. Symptoms: severe headache unresponsive to analgesics, loss of coordination (ataxia — cannot walk a straight line), confusion, altered consciousness, seizures, coma. Ataxia test: ask the soldier to walk heel-to-toe in a straight line. Any difficulty is a red flag. Immediate treatment: descend urgently, supplemental oxygen, Dexamethasone 8mg immediately then 4mg every 6 hours. Hyperbaric bag (Gamow bag) is life-saving if available.
Keywords: HACE, cerebral edema, altitude, ataxia, coordination, Dexamethasone, Gamow bag, seizure, brain swelling

Content: Heat Exhaustion: heavy sweating, weakness, cold/pale/clammy skin, fast weak pulse, nausea, fainting. Core temperature usually below 40°C. Treatment: move to cool area, loosen clothing, apply cool wet cloths, hydrate with ORS. Heat Stroke: high body temperature (above 40°C), hot/red/dry or damp skin, rapid strong pulse, confusion, unconsciousness. This is a medical emergency. Treatment: call for evacuation, rapidly cool the casualty by any means — ice packs to neck/armpits/groin, cool water sponging, fan. Heat stroke can cause permanent organ damage and death if not treated immediately.
Keywords: heat stroke, heat exhaustion, hyperthermia, ORS, dehydration, desert, Rajasthan, summer, hot weather

Content: Malaria is a serious risk in jungle postings — Northeast India, Andaman Islands, and border areas. Caused by Plasmodium parasites via Anopheles mosquito bites (mostly at night). Symptoms: high fever with chills and sweating in cycles, headache, muscle aches, vomiting. Falciparum malaria (most dangerous type) can cause cerebral malaria, kidney failure, and death. Prevention: use DEET-based insect repellent, permethrin-treated uniforms, sleep under mosquito nets, take prophylactic medication as prescribed. Report any fever after jungle exposure immediately to medical staff.
Keywords: malaria, mosquito, jungle, fever, chills, DEET, prophylaxis, Northeast India, Andaman, falciparum

Content: India has the highest snakebite mortality in the world. Venomous snakes soldiers may encounter: King Cobra, Indian Cobra, Common Krait, Russell's Viper, Saw-Scaled Viper. Signs of envenomation: local swelling and pain (viper), drooping eyelids and difficulty swallowing (cobra/krait), bleeding from gums. Do NOT: cut and suck the wound, apply tourniquet, apply electric shock. DO: immobilize the limb below heart level, keep the casualty calm, mark the edge of swelling and time, evacuate urgently to a facility with antivenom. Pressure immobilization bandage is only recommended for neurotoxic snakes (cobra, krait).
Keywords: snakebite, venomous snake, cobra, krait, viper, antivenom, jungle, field medicine, envenomation

Content: Scrub typhus is increasingly common in Indian troops posted in forested areas. Caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi, transmitted by larval mites (chiggers). A classic sign is an eschar — a painless black scab at the bite site, often in hidden areas (groin, armpit, behind ear). Symptoms: sudden high fever, severe headache, rash, enlarged lymph nodes. Untreated, it can cause pneumonia, encephalitis, and organ failure. Treatment: Doxycycline 100mg twice daily for 7-14 days. Always check for eschar during medical examination after jungle operations.
Keywords: scrub typhus, mite, eschar, jungle fever, Doxycycline, forest, chigger, fever, rash

Content: The INSAS (Indian Small Arms System) is the standard infantry rifle of the Indian Army. Caliber: 5.56x45mm NATO. Effective range: 400m (point target), 600m (area target). Magazine capacity: 20 or 30 rounds. Modes: single shot and 3-round burst (no fully automatic). Function check: remove magazine, cock weapon, press trigger (should release), apply safety, press trigger (should not release), decock. Common malfunction: double feed — clear by removing magazine, pulling charging handle twice, visually inspect chamber, reload.
Keywords: INSAS, rifle, 5.56mm, infantry, malfunction, function check, charging handle, magazine, small arms

Content: The AK-203 is being inducting into Indian Army replacing the INSAS. Caliber: 7.62x39mm. Effective range: 400m. Magazine: 30 rounds. Fully automatic capable. Manufactured at Amethi under Indo-Russian joint venture. The AK platform is known for reliability in adverse conditions including sand, mud, and extreme cold — suitable for Ladakh and desert operations. Malfunction clearance (immediate action): tap the magazine, rack the charging handle, observe the chamber, release the bolt, squeeze the trigger (TROSS drills).
Keywords: AK-203, AK47, Kalashnikov, rifle, 7.62mm, Amethi, induction, automatic, malfunction, TROSS

Content: The Dragunov SVD is the primary sniper rifle in Indian Army service. Caliber: 7.62x54mmR. Effective range: 800m (with optical sight), 1300m (maximum). Magazine: 10 rounds, semi-automatic. Equipped with PSO-1 telescopic sight with rangefinder reticle and infrared filter for detecting IR sources. Used extensively in counter-insurgency operations in J&K and counter-sniper roles along the LOC. Windage and elevation adjustments critical for high-altitude engagements due to thinner air and changed ballistics.
Keywords: Dragunov, SVD, sniper rifle, 7.62mm, scope, optical sight, LOC, Kashmir, counter-sniper, long range

Content: The IWI NEGEV LMG is used by Indian Army special forces and some infantry units. Caliber: 5.56x45mm. Belt fed or magazine. Rate of fire: 850-1000 rpm cyclic. Effective range: 1000m. The LMG provides suppressive fire to allow the rifle section to maneuver. Key principles: the LMG gunner should control burst length (5-7 round bursts) to prevent barrel overheating and maintain accuracy. Barrel changes are mandatory after sustained fire.
Keywords: LMG, light machine gun, NEGEV, suppressive fire, belt fed, fire support, infantry section

Content: The Carl Gustaf (84mm) is a man-portable anti-armor and multi-role weapon used extensively by Indian Army infantry. It fires multiple warhead types: HEAT (anti-armor), HE (anti-personnel), ILLUM (illumination), SMOKE, and HEDP. Effective range: 300m vs armor, 700m vs area targets. Backblast danger zone: 100m arc, 60 degrees behind the weapon — never position personnel or flammable materials here. The Carl Gustaf was extensively used in the 1999 Kargil War against Pakistani bunkers.
Keywords: Carl Gustaf, 84mm, recoilless rifle, anti-armor, HEAT, backblast, Kargil, bunker busting, infantry, RPG alternative

Content: The 81mm mortar provides indirect fire support at the battalion level. Maximum range: 5,650m. Minimum range: 100m. Rate of fire: 15 rounds per minute (sustained: 4 rpm). Crew: 3-4 soldiers. Components: barrel, bipod, baseplate, sight. Key safety: misfired round — wait 2 minutes, then carefully extract (never look down the barrel). The mortar is the infantry battalion commander's closest artillery support. Used for suppression, illumination, and smoke missions.
Keywords: mortar, 81mm, indirect fire, artillery, baseplate, bipod, fire support, infantry battalion, illumination, smoke

Content: Topographic maps used by Indian Army are typically at 1:50,000 scale (2cm = 1km on ground). Grid references: 6-digit (100m accuracy), 8-digit (10m accuracy), 10-digit (1m accuracy). Always read grid references: RIGHT then UP (Easting then Northing). Contour lines show elevation — closer lines mean steeper terrain. Index contours (darker, labeled) appear every 5th line. A closed set of contours forming a circle indicates a hilltop. A 'V' shape in contours pointing uphill indicates a valley; pointing downhill indicates a ridge.
Keywords: map reading, navigation, grid reference, topographic map, contour lines, easting, northing, terrain analysis, 1:50000

Content: The prismatic compass is standard issue in the Indian Army. Magnetic bearing: reading from compass. Grid bearing: reading from map. Magnetic declination in India varies by region (check map margin). Converting map bearing to compass bearing: add the magnetic declination if west, subtract if east. Resection: take bearings to two or more known landmarks, plot back bearings on map — your position is where the lines intersect. Always check compass away from metal objects, weapons, and electronic equipment which cause deviation.
Keywords: compass, bearing, navigation, prismatic compass, magnetic declination, resection, back bearing, field craft

Content: The Indian Army uses GPS receivers and increasingly NAVIC (Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System). Key skills: entering waypoints, tracking to a destination, understanding accuracy limitations (especially in deep jungle or mountainous terrain where satellite view is obstructed). Battery conservation: GPS should supplement, not replace map and compass skills. In electronic warfare environments, GPS may be jammed — soldiers must be confident in traditional navigation. MGRS (Military Grid Reference System) is the standard coordinate format for military GPS.
Keywords: GPS, NAVIC, navigation, satellite, MGRS, waypoint, electronic warfare, jamming, battery, digital navigation

Content: Effective camouflage disrupts shape, shine, shadow, silhouette, and movement (5 S's). Shine: camouflage cream on all exposed skin, dull metallic parts with tape or cam cream. Shape: break up outline by adding local vegetation to helmet and clothing. Shadow: avoid standing in doorways or against light backgrounds. Silhouette: never stand on skylines. Movement: slow deliberate movement, freeze when in doubt, move at dusk or night when possible. In snow terrain (Ladakh, Siachen): white over-suits mandatory, avoid dark shadows cast by the body.
Keywords: camouflage, concealment, 5 S's, silhouette, shadow, fieldcraft, snow camouflage, Siachen, observation

Content: Indian Army patrol orders follow the SMEAC format: Situation (enemy and friendly forces), Mission (clear concise statement beginning with 'To' and containing who, what, where, when, why), Execution (scheme of maneuver, tasks to subordinates, coordinating instructions), Administration & Logistics (casualty evacuation, resupply, communications), Command & Signal (chain of command, radio frequencies, codewords, challenge and password). Rehearsals must cover Actions-on: enemy contact, casualty, getting lost, compromise.
Keywords: SMEAC, patrol orders, mission briefing, operations order, execution, admin, command and signal, patrolling

Content: On ambush: if caught in the killing zone — assault through or extract, do not freeze. Battle drills: section attack — Locate, Win the firefight, Attack, Consolidate (LWAC). On contact, the section commander shouts 'CONTACT!' followed by direction using clock method ('Contact 11 o'clock!'). Fire and movement: one group fires while the other moves — bounding overwatch. Never all move at the same time. On breaking contact: peel drill — last man fires, taps the shoulder of the man in front, moves to the rear, and the drill continues.
Keywords: contact drill, ambush, battle drill, section attack, bounding overwatch, peel drill, break contact, immediate action, firefight

Content: Hydration is critical — dehydration degrades performance significantly before thirst is felt. Sources: streams, rain, dew, plant water. Signs of contamination: cloudy water, dead animals nearby, discolored vegetation. Purification methods: boiling (100°C for 1 minute, 3 minutes above 2000m altitude), iodine/chlorine tablets (issued in field kit, wait 30 minutes), water purification filters. Soldier's water consumption: minimum 2-3L/day in temperate conditions, 4-6L/day in desert or heavy exertion. Never share canteens — risk of disease transmission.
Keywords: water, hydration, purification, dehydration, survival, iodine tablets, boiling, canteen, field craft

Content: In a survival situation, food is less critical than water and shelter — a fit soldier can survive 3 weeks without food. Universal edibility test: touch plant to lips (wait 15 min), chew without swallowing (wait 15 min), eat small amount and wait 8 hours — proceed if no ill effects. Avoid: brightly colored plants, plants with milky sap, plants that smell of almonds (cyanide indicator), any mushrooms unless 100% certain. In India: coconut, bamboo shoots, roots of cattail, and most fruits that animals are seen eating are generally safe.
Keywords: survival, foraging, food procurement, edibility test, jungle, emergency

Content: Priority order in survival: Protection, Location, Water, Food (PLWF). Shelter protects from hypothermia, hyperthermia, and insects. Types: lean-to (simplest — requires only one upright support and covering material), A-frame, debris hut (mound of leaves providing insulation — effective in cold environments). Site selection: avoid flood plains, ant nests, dead wood above that could fall, areas with large animal tracks. Insulate from the ground — ground steals body heat 25x faster than air.
Keywords: shelter, survival, lean-to, debris hut, protection, hypothermia, cold weather, field craft

Content: Standard Indian Army radio procedure: Call sign of station called, then own call sign, then message, then 'Over' (reply expected) or 'Out' (conversation ended — never 'Over and Out'). Phonetic alphabet (NATO): Alpha, Bravo, Charlie, Delta, Echo, Foxtrot, Golf, Hotel, India, Juliet, Kilo, Lima, Mike, November, Oscar, Papa, Quebec, Romeo, Sierra, Tango, Uniform, Victor, Whiskey, X-ray, Yankee, Zulu. Numbers spoken digit by digit (e.g., 37 = 'Three Seven'). Radio silence: maintain unless operationally essential.
Keywords: radio procedure, communications, call sign, phonetic alphabet, NATO alphabet, radio silence, Over, Out, signals

Content: Operational Security (OPSEC): Never transmit unit strength, location, or future plans in clear (unencrypted). Avoid patterns in communications that can be exploited by signals intelligence. Mobile phone use near operational areas is restricted — smartphones can reveal location through GPS metadata and towers. Social media posting of operational information is strictly prohibited and a punishable offense. Enemy SIGINT (Signals Intelligence) units actively monitor frequencies. Use encrypted radios (AREN series in Indian Army) for all sensitive communications.
Keywords: OPSEC, encryption, signals intelligence, SIGINT, mobile phone, social media, communications security, radio, AREN

Content: The BMP-2 Sarath is the primary infantry fighting vehicle (IFV) of the Indian Army, manufactured indigenously at Ordnance Factory Medak. It carries up to 7 infantry soldiers plus 3 crew. Armament: 30mm 2A42 autocannon, 7.62mm PKT coaxial machine gun, Konkurs ATGM launcher. It is amphibious — can cross water obstacles by swimming. Crew roles: commander, gunner, driver. The driver uses periscopes in closed-down mode. Soldiers must know emergency exit procedures from all hatches.
Keywords: BMP-2, Sarath, IFV, infantry fighting vehicle, Medak, Konkurs, amphibious, 30mm, armored vehicle

Content: The T-90 Bhishma is the primary Main Battle Tank (MBT) of the Indian Army. Over 1,000 in service. Armament: 125mm smoothbore gun (can fire APFSDS, HEAT, and Refleks ATGM through barrel), 7.62mm coaxial MG, 12.7mm anti-aircraft MG. Engine: 1000hp diesel. Speed: 60 km/h road, 35 km/h off-road. Crew: 3 (commander, gunner, driver). Features: Kontakt-5 explosive reactive armor (ERA), Shtora active protection system. The T-90 was deployed extensively in Rajasthan desert operations and along northern borders.
Keywords: T-90, Bhishma, main battle tank, MBT, 125mm, ERA, Kontakt, Shtora, armor, Rajasthan

Content: Operators must conduct daily PMCS (Preventive Maintenance Checks and Services). Daily checks: fuel level, coolant level, oil level, battery terminals, tyre pressure, lights, brake check. If a vehicle is immobilized in combat: push it off the road if possible, mark its location on map, report up the chain, do not abandon it without authorization. Common field recovery: tow ropes, snatch blocks for mechanical advantage, sand channels for soft ground recovery. Never attempt to work under a vehicle supported only by a jack — use stands.
Keywords: PMCS, vehicle maintenance, recovery, tow rope, daily checks, breakdown, field maintenance

Content: Searching personnel in counter-insurgency: always have two soldiers — one searches, one covers with weapon ready. Search from head to toe: hat/turban, collar, torso, waistband, pockets, crotch area, down both legs, footwear. All jewelry and belt items checked. Vehicle search: engine bay, under chassis, door panels, beneath seats, spare tyre, fuel tank area. Never take eyes off the person being searched. Common IED concealment: inside walls, under road surface, inside animals, buried under culverts.
Keywords: search procedure, counter-insurgency, CI ops, IED search, vehicle search, body search, Kashmir, insurgency

Content: A cordon and search isolates a target area while a search party enters. Key elements: Outer cordon (prevents escape or reinforcement), Inner cordon (immediate perimeter of target building), Search party (systematic search of buildings), Reserve (ready to reinforce). Sequence: establish outer cordon covertly, move inner cordon into position, search party enters. Timing: often pre-dawn (0300-0500) to maximize surprise. All personnel accounted for before withdrawal. Lessons from Kashmir operations: always check rooftops, tunnels, and false walls.
Keywords: cordon and search, C&S, counter-insurgency, Kashmir, J&K, operational tactics, outer cordon, inner cordon, CASO

Content: Improvised Explosive Devices (IEDs) are the primary threat in counter-insurgency. Indicators: disturbed earth, unusual objects on road (stones, plastic bags, wire), dead animals, parked vehicles unattended. Command wire IEDs: look for wire running away from road into vegetation. Vehicle-Borne IEDs (VBIEDs): suspect any vehicle parked unusually or abandoned. On suspected IED: stop, assess, report, keep clear (minimum 300m), inform EOD. Avoid using radios within 25m — can trigger radio-controlled IEDs. Cordon the area and wait for EOD.
Keywords: IED, improvised explosive device, VBIED, command wire, EOD, bomb disposal, counter-insurgency, J&K, recognition, response

Content: The 1999 Kargil War highlighted several tactical lessons. Pakistani forces occupied high-ground features in Dras, Batalik, Mushkoh, and Kaksar sectors. Key lessons: importance of all-weather surveillance and aerial reconnaissance, danger of predictable patrol routes, need for dedicated high-altitude warfare training, critical role of artillery (Bofors guns decisive), need for night-vision equipment. Indian Army's Totak feature and Tiger Hill operations demonstrated successful combined arms coordination. Siachin experience proved vital for troops adapted to extreme altitude.
Keywords: Kargil, 1999, Kargil War, Tiger Hill, Bofors, artillery, high altitude warfare, LOC, tactical lessons

Content: NBC (Nuclear, Biological, Chemical) threats require protective equipment: NBC suit, gloves, and S10 or similar respirator. Decontamination drill: remove contaminated clothing (careful not to touch skin), rinse skin with large amounts of water, proceed to decontamination station. Nerve agent signs: small pupils (miosis), excessive saliva, sweating, muscle twitching, seizures. Antidote: Atropine auto-injector (inject into outer thigh through clothing in emergency). Blister agent (mustard): no immediate pain — symptoms appear 2-24 hours later, causing blisters and eye damage.
Keywords: NBC, CBRN, chemical, biological, nuclear, decontamination, nerve agent, atropine, respirator, protection

Content: Detection methods in Indian Army include: M8 paper (turns yellow-green for nerve/blister agents), M9 tape (turns pink/red/red-purple in presence of liquid agents), electronic detectors. Water test for blister agents: drop water on suspected area — mustard forms a distinct oily bead. On first suspicion of chemical attack: immediately don mask, give alarm (three blasts on whistle, shout 'Gas! Gas! Gas!'), don gloves and suit in correct order. Do not remove mask until cleared by NBC NCO with detection equipment.
Keywords: chemical detection, M8 paper, M9 tape, gas mask, NBC alarm, blister agent, nerve agent, CBRN detection

Content: The Army Act 1950 governs the Indian Army. Key sections relevant to soldiers: Section 39 (absence without leave), Section 40 (desertion), Section 41 (striking a superior officer), Section 45 (disgraceful conduct), Section 63 (prejudice to military discipline — residual clause). Punishment types: Summary punishment (awarded by Commanding Officer) and Court Martial (General, District, Summary General, Summary). Soldiers have the right to submit a complaint through the chain of command under Army Rules 177. Redressal mechanism: complaint to CO, then formation commander, then Army Headquarters.
Keywords: Army Act 1950, discipline, AWOL, absence without leave, desertion, court martial, summary punishment, soldier rights, complaint, CO

Content: Promotion in Indian Army for Other Ranks (ORs): Recruit to Sepoy (on completion of training), Sepoy to Naik (by selection/time scale after minimum service), Naik to Havildar, Havildar to Naib Subedar (JCO — major promotion gate), Naib Subedar to Subedar, Subedar to Subedar Major (highest JCO rank). Annual Confidential Reports (ACRs) are critical. Physical fitness tests (including running, pull-ups, and combat fitness) are mandatory for promotion boards. LDMC (Lower Divisional Military Course) and other qualifying courses are required.
Keywords: promotion, ACR, confidential report, Naik, Havildar, Subedar, JCO, other ranks, sepoy, career progression

Content: Key welfare schemes for soldiers and veterans: ECHS (Ex-Servicemen Contributory Health Scheme) — healthcare for ESM and dependents at ECHS polyclinics and empanelled hospitals. Canteen Stores Department (CSD) — subsidized goods. OROP (One Rank One Pension) — uniform pension for same rank and service. Sainik Boards at district, state, and central level for welfare issues. Army Wives Welfare Association (AWWA). PM Scholarship for children of deceased/disabled soldiers. Veterans India portal for online grievance redressal.
Keywords: ECHS, welfare, OROP, CSD, canteen, veterans, pension, Sainik Board, AWWA, ex-servicemen

Topic: Casualty and Next of Kin Procedures
Content: In the event of a soldier becoming a casualty: immediate informing of unit Adjutant, who informs the Commanding Officer. Official communication to family via Record Office — do not allow unofficial information to reach family first. Battle casualty: ex-gratia payment, pension continuation, disability pension (if applicable). Next of Kin (NOK) details must be kept updated in service documents. If a soldier dies in action, the family receives Veer Nari (war widow) status with associated benefits including priority government housing.
Keywords: casualty, next of kin, NOK, war widow, Veer Nari, battle casualty, death in service, compensation, record office, pension

Content: Combat stress is a normal response to abnormal situations. Immediate stress reactions (during/just after combat): confusion, trembling, tears, aggression. Delayed reactions (weeks-months later): flashbacks, nightmares, hypervigilance, emotional numbing, avoidance — this is Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD). The Indian Army increasingly has formal mental health support through NIMHANS collaboration and unit medical officers. It is NOT a sign of weakness — it is a physiological injury. Soldiers experiencing these symptoms must report to the MO. Early treatment significantly improves outcomes.
Keywords: PTSD, combat stress, mental health, flashback, nightmare, hypervigilance, Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder, psychological, battle fatigue, MO

Content: The Indian Army has a Buddy System where soldiers are paired to watch out for each other's mental and physical wellbeing. Warning signs a buddy may need help: giving away possessions, saying goodbye in a final way, sudden calmness after depression, withdrawal from unit activities, dramatic mood changes, talking about feeling worthless. Action: engage in conversation, listen without judgment, immediately inform the section commander and MO — this is the correct action, not a betrayal. All such cases are handled with confidentiality.
Keywords: buddy system, suicide prevention, mental health, welfare, warning signs, section commander, MO, confidentiality, soldier welfare

Content: Nuclear detonation hazards: blast, heat, initial radiation, fallout (residual radiation). Protective measures: distance (most effective), shielding (dense materials — concrete, earth, lead absorb gamma), time (reduce exposure duration). In a fallout area: stay indoors in brick/concrete building, seal gaps with wet cloth, switch off ventilation, remove outer clothing before entering shelter (removes 80% of contamination). Dosimetry: radiation dose measured in mSv/Gy. Army issues personal dosimeters to track cumulative dose. KI (Potassium Iodide) tablets protect thyroid from radioactive iodine — only effective if taken before or just after exposure.
Keywords: radiation, nuclear, fallout, dosimeter, shielding, potassium iodide, KI tablet, CBRN, gamma radiation, detonation

Content: Basic mountaineering skills are essential for high-altitude operations in Jammu & Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh, and Uttarakhand. Rappelling (abseiling): controlled descent on rope. Key knots: Figure-8 (anchor), Bowline (harness tie-in), Clove hitch (quick attachment), Prussik (self-arrest). Three points of contact always maintained when climbing. Belay system: one person always anchored and protecting the climber. Equipment: carabiners, belay devices, harness, helmet, pitons. The High Altitude Warfare School (HAWS) at Gulmarg trains soldiers in these skills.
Keywords: mountaineering, rappelling, rock climbing, HAWS, Gulmarg, knots, belay, carabiner, high altitude, mountain warfare

Content: Avalanche risk is constant in Siachen, Kargil, and high-altitude postings — the Indian Army loses more soldiers to avalanches annually than to enemy action. High-risk conditions: recent heavy snowfall (within 24 hours), temperature rise, wind-loaded slopes, convex slope features, previous avalanche debris. If caught in an avalanche: discard equipment, fight to stay on surface (swimming motion), cover mouth with arm as snow settles, create an air pocket before snow stops, try to push hand to surface. Bury a glove as marker. Rescue: buried victims have 90% survival if found in 15 minutes, dropping rapidly after 45 minutes.
Keywords: avalanche, Siachen, snow, high altitude, survival, rescue, avalanche beacon, winter warfare, Kargil

Content: Room clearing is a dangerous task requiring precision. Four-man team standard: number 1 and 2 make entry (cross immediately to opposite corners), number 3 and 4 follow to clear remaining corners. Never stack against the door (fatal funnel). Grenade: shout 'GRENADE!' before throwing into room, take cover, enter after detonation — 'Clear!' call. Pie the door: incrementally clear visible area before entry. Clearing direction: always work from the dominant corner. Communicate constantly: 'Covering left!', 'Moving right!', 'Room clear!'. In Indian context practiced extensively for Kashmir counter-insurgency operations.
Keywords: room clearing, urban warfare, FIBUA, close quarter battle, CQB, fatal funnel, grenade, Kashmir, infantry tactics

Content: FIBUA (Fighting In Built-Up Areas) principles: move along walls not across open streets, use shadows, avoid windows (silhouette danger), use multiple routes, communicate with hand signals when possible. Rooftops: valuable observation but dangerous — silhouette against sky. Breach: door (kick, ram, explosive), wall (sledgehammer, C2 explosive, vehicle). Dominate the high ground — control of upper floors is critical. Civilian presence: strict ROE (Rules of Engagement) must be followed. Collateral damage prevention is operational, legal, and moral imperative. Lessons from Srinagar operations extensively documented by Indian Army.
Keywords: FIBUA, urban warfare, built-up area, Srinagar, ROE, rules of engagement, civilians, rooftop, breach, hand signals

Content: Important battles and operations in Indian Army history: Battle of Longewala (1971) — small force held Pakistan armor with air support (basis of movie Border). Operation Vijay (1961) — Goa liberation. Operation Blue Star (1984) — Golden Temple. Operation Pawan (1987-1990) — Sri Lanka IPKF. Operation Vijay (1999) — Kargil War. Operation Parakram (2001-2002) — mobilization after Parliament attack. Operation Rakshak — ongoing counter-insurgency J&K. PVC (Param Vir Chakra) — India's highest gallantry award, equivalent to Victoria Cross/Medal of Honor.
Keywords: Longewala, Battle Honours, Operation Vijay, Kargil, Operation Pawan, IPKF, Param Vir Chakra, PVC, gallantry, Indian Army history

Content: Indian Army ranks from lowest to highest: Sepoy/Gunner/Sapper/Trooper, Lance Naik, Naik, Havildar, Naib Subedar (JCO), Subedar (JCO), Subedar Major/Risaldar Major (JCO), 2nd Lieutenant, Lieutenant, Captain, Major, Lieutenant Colonel, Colonel, Brigadier, Major General, Lieutenant General, General, Field Marshal (honorary, wartime). JCOs (Junior Commissioned Officers) are a unique Indian Army institution — senior to soldiers but subordinate to commissioned officers. They are the backbone of the regiment.
Keywords: rank structure, Sepoy, Naik, Havildar, JCO, Subedar, officer ranks, Lieutenant, General, regimental

Content: A standard Indian Army infantry battalion has approximately 850 soldiers. Structure: Battalion HQ, HQ Company, 4 Rifle Companies (Alpha/Bravo/Charlie/Delta). Each Rifle Company: Company HQ + 3 Platoons. Each Platoon: Platoon HQ + 3 Sections. Each Section: 10 soldiers (Section Commander + 9 soldiers). Support elements: Mortar Platoon, Anti-Tank Platoon, Signal Platoon, Pioneer Platoon, Reconnaissance Platoon. Commanding Officer is a Colonel; Company Commanders are Majors; Platoon Commanders are Lieutenants or 2nd Lieutenants.
Keywords: battalion, infantry organisation, company, platoon, section, CO, OC, platoon commander, structure, army organisation

Content: Unconscious breathing casualty: place in recovery position (on their side, upper knee bent forward to prevent rolling, lower arm behind, upper arm supporting head). This prevents choking on vomit or blood. Airway obstruction: look (chest rise), listen (breath sounds), feel (air on cheek). If obstructed: head tilt-chin lift, jaw thrust (if spinal injury suspected). Choking (conscious): 5 back blows between shoulder blades, then 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver). If unconscious: begin CPR. Jaw thrust is used in trauma as it does not move the cervical spine.
Keywords: recovery position, airway, unconscious, choking, Heimlich, jaw thrust, head tilt, CPR, first aid, trauma

Content: Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) for a soldier with no pulse and not breathing: 30 compressions to 2 rescue breaths (30:2 ratio). Compression depth: at least 5cm (2 inches), at the center of the chest. Rate: 100-120 compressions per minute (tempo: 'Stayin' Alive'). Allow full chest recoil. If untrained or reluctant: compression-only CPR (no mouth-to-mouth) is effective. Continue CPR until: casualty regains pulse and breathing, trained medical personnel take over, you are too exhausted to continue, or evacuation to definitive care. AED use: apply pads as shown in diagram, follow voice prompts.
Keywords: CPR, cardiopulmonary resuscitation, pulse, compressions, rescue breaths, AED, cardiac arrest, first aid, field medicine

Topic: Field Radio Types in Indian Army
Content: Radio sets commonly used in Indian Army: AREN (Army Radio Engineered Net) series — secure encrypted tactical radios. Harris RF-7800 — software defined radio, used by special forces. Motorola TETRA radios — for peacekeeping and urban ops. VHF radios: line-of-sight, 30-300MHz, range 10-20km depending on terrain. HF radios: can communicate over the horizon using skywave propagation, 3-30MHz, used for long-range communication. BFSR-SR and other battlefield surveillance radars also have associated communication. Always check battery level, antenna connection, and frequency before operation.
Keywords: radio, AREN, VHF, HF, communications, signals, frequency, encryption, antenna, tactical radio

Content: Indian Army field rations (composite rations) provide approximately 3,600-4,200 kcal per day for medium-intensity operations (4,500+ kcal for high-altitude and cold weather). Components: carbohydrates (main fuel — chapati, rice, biscuits), proteins (dal, meat products), fats, vitamins. At altitude, appetite is naturally suppressed — soldiers must be encouraged/ordered to eat. Dehydration mimics altitude sickness — drink before you feel thirsty. Avoid alcohol at altitude as it accelerates dehydration and impairs judgment. Hot rations boost morale significantly.
Keywords: ration, nutrition, calories, composite ration, high altitude, appetite, hydration, carbohydrates, protein, field feeding

Content: CASEVAC (Casualty Evacuation) is the movement of casualties from point of injury to medical facility. 9-Line MEDEVAC request: Line 1-Location (grid), Line 2-Radio frequency/callsign, Line 3-Number of patients by precedence (P1 Urgent, P2 Priority, P3 Routine), Line 4-Special equipment needed, Line 5-Number by type (litter/ambulatory), Line 6-Security at pickup site, Line 7-Method of marking, Line 8-Patient nationality and status, Line 9-NBC contamination. In Indian Army, Dhruv helicopter (ALH-MR) is primary CASEVAC platform in high-altitude areas.
Keywords: CASEVAC, MEDEVAC, 9-line, evacuation, helicopter, Dhruv, ALH, medical evacuation, casualty, P1 P2 P3

Content: India shares borders with Pakistan (3,323 km), China (3,488 km — Line of Actual Control), Bangladesh (4,156 km), Nepal (1,751 km), Bhutan (699 km), Myanmar (1,643 km), and a maritime boundary with Sri Lanka and Maldives. Key contested areas: LOC (Line of Control) with Pakistan in J&K, LAC (Line of Actual Control) with China in Ladakh/Arunachal Pradesh. The Siachen Glacier is the world's highest battlefield at 5,400m average elevation. Depsang, Galwan, Pangong Tso, and Daulat Beg Oldie are key areas of concern along LAC.
Keywords: border, LOC, LAC, Pakistan, China, Siachen, Ladakh, Arunachal, Galwan, Pangong

Content: Night Vision Devices (NVDs) used by Indian Army include image intensifier (Gen 2 and Gen 3) goggles and monoculars, and thermal imaging sights. Image intensifiers amplify available light — not effective in complete darkness. Thermal imagers detect heat signature — effective in all light conditions. Key limitations: NVDs impair depth perception and narrow field of view — soldiers must be trained to compensate. Battery life critical — always carry spare batteries. Care: never expose NVDs to bright light when switched on, keep dry and free of lens condensation. IRGSU (Individual Rifle Grenade Sight Unit) and BEL-made products are Indian.
Keywords: night vision, NVD, thermal imaging, image intensifier, Gen 2, Gen 3, low light, darkness, surveillance, BEL

Content: Rules of Engagement (ROE) are directives from military authority that define when, where, and how force may be used. In J&K operations, soldiers operate under AFSPA (Armed Forces Special Powers Act) which grants specific powers in disturbed areas. Key principle: force must be necessary and proportionate. Escalation of Force (EOF): shout warning, show weapon, non-lethal warning (flare/warning shot), lethal force — only if threat to life is imminent. Any use of lethal force must be reported up the chain immediately. Weapon must not be fired in anger — always an ordered act.
Keywords: ROE, rules of engagement, AFSPA, use of force, escalation of force, lethal force, proportionate, J&K, disturbed area, legal
